title: Finite-frequency anomaly-induced electromechanical response of Dirac fermions in deformed graphene
abstract: A deformation of a graphene sheet changes more than the positions of the atoms. In the low-energy Dirac theory it also produces geometric electron-phonon vertices. One of these vertices acts as an emergent phonon gauge field, $\calA_μ$, which couples to the same Dirac current as the electromagnetic vector potential. This shared current vertex gives a direct route from mechanics to electronics: a moving deformation can generate a transverse electric current, and a deformation pattern with emergent phonon flux can bind electric charge. We show that the coefficient of this mixed electromechanical response is the parity-odd current-current correlator of a massive Dirac cone. For an insulating cone the coefficient is the one-cone Chern-Simons value, while for a doped cone in the local regime it is reduced by the Berry curvature factor $m/|μ|$. We apply the response to explicit deformations. A traveling flexural wave generates a transverse second-harmonic current; a static ripple mixed with a dynamic phonon generates a transverse current at the drive frequency; and two non-collinear modes can generate charge modulation through the emergent phonon flux. We keep the spin and valley sum explicit, so the paper shows when the one-cone anomaly becomes a charge current in graphene and when it instead appears in a valley, spin, or spin-valley channel. For sublattice-gapped graphene with a valley-odd deformation gauge coupling, the two valleys add rather than cancel. The experimentally sharp signature is a transverse electrical signal at twice the flexural-wave frequency, with a phase fixed by the sign of the sublattice gap and a gate dependence that crosses over from a gap plateau to a $1/|μ|$ decay. These direction, phase, frequency, and gate-voltage selection rules give clean tests of the anomaly-induced electromechanical channel in deformed graphene.
summary: This paper demonstrates that mechanical deformations in graphene can generate transverse electrical currents through a parity-odd anomaly. It provides specific experimental signatures, such as frequency-doubled signals and gate-dependent decay, which allow for the direct detection of emergent gauge fields in deformed 2D materials.
main_problem: Investigating the electromechanical response of Dirac fermions in deformed graphene induced by a finite-frequency anomaly and identifying experimental signatures to distinguish this from ordinary deformation-potential backgrounds.
main_result: The paper derives a mixed Chern-Simons relation where mechanical deformations generate a transverse electric current, predicting specific frequency harmonics (2w) and gate-voltage dependencies (1/|mu| decay) as clean experimental fingerprints.
method: The author uses a continuum Dirac theory framework, evaluating the one-loop parity-odd current-current correlator and performing spin and valley summation to determine the observable charge response.
model_or_system: The system consists of deformed, sublattice-asymmetric graphene (e.g., on hBN) modeled by low-energy Dirac fermions on a curved manifold with an emergent phonon gauge field.
key_observables: Transverse electric current, second-harmonic current (2w), charge modulation, and the effective phonon gauge field flux.
important_parameters: Dirac mass (m), chemical potential (mu), deformation gauge coupling (lambda_A), and frequency (omega).
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes the weak-deformation limit, the continuum limit of the tight-binding model, and the one-loop approximation for the fermion-phonon interaction.
figures_summary: Fig 1 illustrates local geometry and geometric gauge fields; Fig 2 shows the Feynman diagram for the anomaly-induced signal and compares it to other vertices.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the geometric origin of the phonon gauge field, derives the mixed Chern-Simons response via one-loop calculations, analyzes the response in different doping regimes, and concludes with specific experimental selection rules and symmetry-based detection protocols.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Local geometry of a deformed honeycomb sheet. (a) A deformed lattice is described by local patches, here denoted U1 and U2, which cover neighboring lattice sites and the link between them. The patch language keeps track of how the curved or distorted sheet is assembled from local coordinate systems. (b) A site at position ⃗X(ˆξ) in three-dimensional space is rotated into a local flat reference plane. These local rotations are the microscopic source of the geometric gauge fields that appear in the continuum Dirac theory.

FIG. 3. (Color online) In real space the response has a simple meaning. The phonon flux BA produces charge, while the phonon electric field EA produces a transverse current. The same coefficient C = eλAΠR odd controls both branches.

FIG. 4. (Color online) The nonlinearity of Ai fixes the har- monic content: a single moving flexural wave gives a trans- verse current at 2ω, a static ripple plus a moving phonon gives a transverse current at the drive frequency ω, and two non- collinear modes give charge at ω1 ± ω2 when their emergent phonon flux is nonzero.
